1: The sabbatical year — release every seventh year. The shĕmiṭṭâ extends the Sabbath pattern into economics: debt is released, the land rests, creation breathes. Jesus' proclamation of "the year of the LORD's favor" in Luke 4:18–19 (citing Isaiah 61:1–2, which itself draws on the Jubilee/sabbatical-year legislation) announces the ultimate release — not agricultural debt but the deeper bondage of sin and death. The sabbatical year is the shadow; Christ's proclamation of liberation is the substance. Shadow.
2: Creditors must cancel debts to fellow Israelites — the LORD's release is proclaimed. The mutual release of debt within the covenant community creates a social ecology of grace. Jesus builds the same logic into the Lord's Prayer: "Forgive us our debts, as we also have forgiven our debtors" (Matthew 6:12). The reception of God's forgiveness creates the capacity and obligation to extend forgiveness to others. The sabbatical-year debt release is the economic form of what Christ gives as spiritual reality. Theme.
3: Debts may be collected from foreigners but must be released for fellow Israelites. The covenant community operates under a distinctive economy of grace. In Christ, the community of forgiven-forgivers expands beyond ethnic Israel to include all who are in him: "Bear with each other and forgive one another if any of you has a grievance against someone. Forgive as the Lord forgave you" (Colossians 3:13). The internal economy of grace that once defined the covenant people now defines the church. Theme.
4: "There need be no poor among you." The vision of a community without poverty — contingent on covenant obedience. The early church realizes this vision through the Spirit: "there was not a needy person among them, for as many as were owners of lands or houses sold them" (Acts 4:34 — echoing Deuteronomy 15:4 directly). Luke's explicit echo of Deuteronomy's language in Acts marks the church as the community where the covenant vision becomes reality through Christ and the Spirit. Direct NT echo (Acts 4:34).
5: "If only you carefully obey the voice of the LORD your God." The covenant vision is conditioned on obedience the law cannot guarantee. The "if only" signals the law's own limitation — it states the condition it cannot itself fulfill. In Christ, this condition is met: he perfectly obeyed, and through the Spirit he empowers his people's obedience. "For God has done what the law, weakened by the flesh, could not do" (Romans 8:3–4). The law's conditional vision becomes the Spirit's accomplished reality. Theme.
6: "The LORD your God will bless you as he has promised, and you will lend to many nations but not borrow." The economic blessing of covenant faithfulness flows to the nations — Israel as the lender rather than the borrower. In Christ, the spiritual blessing flows to the nations: the church is entrusted with the gospel of grace to lend to the whole world — "a light for the Gentiles, that you may bring salvation to the ends of the earth" (Acts 13:47, citing Isaiah 49:6). The covenant blessing moves from the economic to the spiritual register. Theme.
7: "You shall not harden your heart or shut your hand against your poor brother. Instead, you shall open your hand wide." The open hand as the sign of the covenant community. This language recurs in Jesus' teaching: "Give to the one who begs from you, and do not refuse the one who would borrow from you" (Matthew 5:42). James connects the shut hand directly to dead faith (James 2:15–17). The hardened heart toward the poor is the covenant violation that the law condemns and Christ exposes. Theme.
8: "You shall open your hand wide to him and lend him whatever he needs, enough for his lack." The open hand gives generously, enough for the actual need — not a token gesture but sufficient provision. In Christ, the sufficient provision is defined at its highest: "And my God will supply every need of yours according to his riches in glory in Christ Jesus" (Philippians 4:19). The covenant pattern of sufficient provision mirrors the divine character. Revelation of God.
9: "Guard against an unworthy thought in your heart: 'The seventh year, the year of release, is near.'" The law must guard against the calculating refusal of generosity. The Deuteronomic warning anticipates the heart's capacity to weaponize the law — to use the approaching release year as a reason to withhold rather than to give. Jesus addresses this same heart dynamic: "when you give to the needy, do not let your left hand know what your right hand is doing" (Matthew 6:3). The calculating heart that Deuteronomy warns against is the same the Sermon on the Mount addresses. Theme.
10: "Give to him generously, and do not be reluctant when you give, for because of this the LORD your God will bless you in all your work." Generous giving, without inner reluctance, brings divine blessing. Paul applies the same principle: "Whoever sows sparingly will also reap sparingly, and whoever sows bountifully will also reap bountifully... God loves a cheerful giver" (2 Corinthians 9:6–7). The Deuteronomic link between generous giving and divine blessing is the OT foundation for Paul's theology of generosity. Theme.
11: "For there will never cease to be poor people in the land. That is why I am commanding you to open your hand wide to your brother." Jesus directly quotes this verse when defending the woman who anoints him: "For you always have the poor with you, but you will not always have me" (Matthew 26:11; Mark 14:7; John 12:8). The Deuteronomic statement becomes the context for understanding the unrepeatable nature of Christ's presence — the poor are always with us and always deserve care; Christ was uniquely present for a finite period. Direct NT citation.
12: The Hebrew slave released in the seventh year. The slave-release law extends the sabbatical principle to persons — freedom granted at the seventh year, mirroring the debt release of Deuteronomy 15:1–2. Paul uses the slave-freedom language christologically: "For freedom Christ has set us free; stand firm therefore, and do not submit again to a yoke of slavery" (Galatians 5:1). The seventh-year release from indentured service is the type; Christ's liberation from sin's slavery is the substance. Shadow.
13: "When you set him free, you shall not send him away empty-handed." The freed slave goes out with generous provision — not returned to the vulnerability of poverty. In Christ, those set free are sent out with every provision: "His divine power has given us everything we need for a godly life" (2 Peter 1:3). Freedom in Christ is not a bare release but a release into abundance. Revelation of God.
14: "Supply him generously from your flock, your threshing floor, and your winepress. Give to him as the LORD your God has blessed you." The provision for the freed slave mirrors the scale of God's blessing — give proportionally to what you have received. The principle of proportional generosity grounded in divine blessing is the OT basis for Paul's theology: "He who supplies seed to the sower and bread for food will supply and multiply your seed for sowing and increase the harvest of your righteousness" (2 Corinthians 9:10). Theme.
15: "Remember that you were a slave in Egypt, and the LORD your God redeemed you." Memory of redemption grounds ethical action — the redeemed must be redemptive toward others. The NT extends this logic to Christ's redemption: "knowing that you were ransomed... with the precious blood of Christ" (1 Peter 1:18–19) shapes every ethical obligation. The memory of redemption creates the character of the redeemed. Theme.
16: The voluntary permanent servant — who loves his master and chooses not to leave — has his ear pierced against the door. This is the voluntary, love-motivated covenant bond. Psalms 40:6 ("ears you have dug for me; you did not desire burnt offering and sin offering. Then I said, 'Here I am; I have come'") is cited in Hebrews 10:5–7 as the words of Christ at the Incarnation: "a body you have prepared for me... I have come to do your will, O God." The willing servant who chooses permanent bondage out of love for his master is the type Christ embodies at the Incarnation. Direct type (Hebrews 10:5–7).
17: The awl through the ear against the door — a permanent mark of voluntary covenant servitude. The ear piercing enacts permanent, public, irrevocable commitment. Christ's commitment to the Father's will is irrevocable and public: "he humbled himself by becoming obedient to the point of death, even death on a cross" (Philippians 2:8). What the ear-piercing marked for the slave, the cross marked for the Son — permanent, visible, irreversible covenant loyalty. Type.
18: "Do not find it difficult when you set him free, for he has served you six years at half the cost of a hired worker." The law removes the economic complaint against generosity in freedom-giving. The releasing master is to set the slave free without resentment. God sets free without resentment — at infinite cost, through the cross: "God was reconciling the world to himself in Christ, not counting people's sins against them" (2 Corinthians 5:19). The freedom given freely and without reluctance reaches its fullest expression in the cross. Revelation of God.
19: "You shall set apart for the LORD your God every firstborn male born of your herd and your flock." The firstborn male consecrated to YHWH — the first and best dedicated as representative of the whole. Christ is the firstborn in every sense the NT deploys: "the firstborn of all creation" (Colossians 1:15); "the firstborn among many brothers" (Romans 8:29); "the firstborn from the dead" (Colossians 1:18; Revelation 1:5). The firstborn male dedicated to YHWH is the type; Christ as YHWH's consecrated firstborn who gives himself as the definitive offering is the fulfillment. Direct type.
20: The firstborn animal is eaten before the LORD at the central sanctuary — the firstborn offering becomes a covenant feast. The firstborn's dedication and the covenant meal are linked. In Christ, the one who is the firstborn from the dead (Colossians 1:18) is also the host of the covenant meal: "I will not drink again of this fruit of the vine until that day when I drink it new with you in my Father's kingdom" (Matthew 26:29). The firstborn meal anticipates the eschatological feast where Christ himself is both offering and host. Type.
21: "But if it has any defect — if it is lame or blind or has any serious flaw — you do not sacrifice it to the LORD your God." The defect-free requirement is absolute — the sacrifice presented to YHWH must be unblemished. Christ qualifies where every other candidate is disqualified: "a lamb without blemish or spot" (1 Peter 1:19); "through the eternal Spirit offered himself without blemish to God" (Hebrews 9:14). The law that disqualifies every defective offering positively requires the one perfect offering that Christ provides. Direct type.
22: Defective firstborns may be eaten locally by anyone, clean or unclean alike — like a gazelle or deer. The animal that cannot be offered in the sanctuary is still good for food; it is not wasted. The distinction between the sacred and the common does not make the common worthless. In Christ, the entire creation is redeemed for God's glory: "everything created by God is good, and nothing is to be rejected if it is received with thanksgiving" (1 Timothy 4:4). Revelation of God.
23: "Only do not eat its blood; pour it out on the ground like water." The blood prohibition maintains the sanctity of life — blood is life, and life belongs to YHWH. This ground-level prohibition prepares for the central importance of blood in the covenant's sacrificial system and in Christ's atoning work. "Without the shedding of blood there is no forgiveness of sins" (Hebrews 9:22) — the blood that Israel poured on the ground in reverence is the type of the blood Christ shed for the forgiveness that the whole sacrificial system anticipated. Type.
